title: Solve triangles using the law of sines
description: Find missing triangle measures using the law of sines.
topic_path: Solve triangles using the law of sines

assessment_items.question_content:
The following figure shows $\triangle ABC$ with side lengths to the nearest tenth.

[[☃ image 1]]

**Find $AC$ in $\triangle ABC$.**  
Round to the nearest tenth.

$AC={}$[[☃ numeric-input 1]]
hints:
###Strategy

We can use the **law of sines** to relate the side lengths and angle measures of a triangle when we know the measures of either of the following:

- Two sides and a *non-included* angle
- Two angles and any side (though we might need to find the other angle measure)

The law of sines says the following is true for any pair of angles in a triangle:

$\dfrac{\text{length opposite }\angle 1}{\sin(m\angle 1)}=\dfrac{\text{length opposite }\angle 2}{\sin(m\angle 2)}$

###Selecting two angles and their opposite sides

We know that $m\angle C=40\degree$ and that the opposite side measures $12$ units. Side $\overline{AC}$ is opposite $\angle B$, which measures $110\degree$.

###Applying the law of sines

$\begin{align}
\dfrac{AC}{\sin(m\angle B)} &= \dfrac{AB}{\sin(m\angle C)}\\\\
\dfrac{AC}{\sin(110\degree)} &= \dfrac{12}{\sin(40\degree)}\\\\
AC &= \dfrac{12\sin(110\degree)}{\sin(40\degree)}\\\\
AC &\approx 17.5
\end{align}$

###Answer

$AC\approx 17.5$

[[☃ image 1]]

options.alt: Triangle ABC. Side AB measures 12 units. Angle B is 110 degrees and angle C is 40 degrees.

assessment_items.question_content:
The following figure shows $\triangle ABC$ with side lengths to the nearest tenth.

[[☃ image 1]]

**Find $AC$ in $\triangle ABC$.**  
Round to the nearest tenth.

$AC={}$[[☃ numeric-input 1]]
hints:
###Strategy

We can use the **law of sines** to relate the side lengths and angle measures of a triangle when we know the measures of either of the following:

- Two sides and a *non-included* angle
- Two angles and any side (though we might need to find the other angle measure)

The law of sines says the following is true for any pair of angles in a triangle:

$\dfrac{\text{length opposite }\angle 1}{\sin(m\angle 1)}=\dfrac{\text{length opposite }\angle 2}{\sin(m\angle 2)}$

###Selecting two angles and their opposite sides

We know that $m\angle A=125\degree$ and that the opposite side measures $7$ units. Side $\overline{AC}$ is opposite $\angle B$, which measures $29\degree$.

###Applying the law of sines

$\begin{align}
\dfrac{AC}{\sin(m\angle B)} &= \dfrac{BC}{\sin(m\angle A)}\\\\
\dfrac{AC}{\sin(29\degree)} &= \dfrac{7}{\sin(125\degree)}\\\\
AC &= \dfrac{7\sin(29\degree)}{\sin(125\degree)}\\\\
AC &\approx 4.1
\end{align}$

###Answer

$AC\approx 4.1$

[[☃ image 1]]

options.alt: Triangle ABC. Side BC measures 7 units. Angle B is 29 degrees and angle A is 125 degrees.

###Selecting two angles and their opposite sides

We know that $m\angle A=71\degree$ and that the opposite side measures $6$ units. Side $\overline{AB}$ is opposite $\angle C$, which measures $48\degree$.

###Applying the law of sines

$\begin{align}
\dfrac{AB}{\sin(m\angle C)} &= \dfrac{BC}{\sin(m\angle A)}\\\\

$AB\approx 4.7$

[[☃ image 1]]

options.alt: Triangle ABC. Side BC measures 6 units. Angle A is 71 degrees and angle C is 48 degrees.

###Selecting two angles and their opposite sides

We know that $m\angle B=80\degree$ and that the opposite side measures $20$ units. Side $\overline{BC}$ is opposite $\angle A$, which measures $67\degree$.

###Applying the law of sines

$\begin{align}
\dfrac{BC}{\sin(m\angle A)} &= \dfrac{AC}{\sin(m\angle B)}\\\\
\dfrac{BC}{\sin(67\degree)} &= \dfrac{20}{\sin(80\degree)}\\\\

BC &\approx 18.7

assessment_items.question_content:
The following figure shows $\triangle ABC$ with side lengths to the nearest tenth.

[[☃ image 1]]

**Find $AB$ in $\triangle ABC$.**  
Round to the nearest tenth.

$AB={}$[[☃ numeric-input 1]]
hints:
###Strategy

We can use the **law of sines** to relate the side lengths and angle measures of a triangle when we know the measures of either of the following:

- Two sides and a *non-included* angle
- Two angles and any side (though we might need to find the other angle measure)

The law of sines says the following is true for any pair of angles in a triangle:

$\dfrac{\text{length opposite }\angle 1}{\sin(m\angle 1)}=\dfrac{\text{length opposite }\angle 2}{\sin(m\angle 2)}$

###Selecting two angles and their opposite sides

We know that $m\angle A=37\degree$ and that the opposite side measures $7$ units. To solve for $AB$, we'll need the measure of the opposite angle, $\angle C$.

$\begin{align}
m\angle A+m\angle B + m\angle C&=180\degree\\\\
37\degree+ 71\degree + m\angle C&=180\degree\\\\
m\angle C&=72\degree
\end{align}$

###Applying the law of sines

$\begin{align}
\dfrac{BC}{\sin(m\angle A)} &= \dfrac{AB}{\sin(m\angle C)}\\\\
\dfrac{7}{\sin(37\degree)} &= \dfrac{AB}{\sin(72\degree)}\\\\
\dfrac{7\sin(72\degree)}{\sin(37\degree)} &=AB\\\\
11.1&\approx AB
\end{align}$

###Answer

$AB\approx 11.1$

[[☃ image 1]]

options.alt: Triangle ABC. Side BC measures 7 units. Angle A is 37 degrees and angle B is 71 degrees.

assessment_items.question_content:
The following figure shows $\triangle ABC$ with side lengths to the nearest tenth.

[[☃ image 1]]

**Find $AB$ in $\triangle ABC$.**  
Round to the nearest tenth.

$AB={}$[[☃ numeric-input 1]]
hints:
###Strategy

We can use the **law of sines** to relate the side lengths and angle measures of a triangle when we know the measures of either of the following:

- Two sides and a *non-included* angle
- Two angles and any side (though we might need to find the other angle measure)

The law of sines says the following is true for any pair of angles in a triangle:

$\dfrac{\text{length opposite }\angle 1}{\sin(m\angle 1)}=\dfrac{\text{length opposite }\angle 2}{\sin(m\angle 2)}$

###Selecting two angles and their opposite sides

Side $\overline{AB}$ is opposite $\angle C$, which measures $81\degree$. We know that side $\overline{AC}$ measures $10$ units. To apply the law of sines, we'll need the measure of $\angle B$, the angle opposite side $\overline{AC}$.

$\begin{align}
m\angle A+m\angle B + m\angle C&=180\degree\\\\
65\degree+m\angle B + 81\degree&=180\degree\\\\
m\angle B&=34\degree
\end{align}$

###Applying the law of sines

$\begin{align}
\dfrac{AC}{\sin(m\angle B)} &= \dfrac{AB}{\sin(m\angle C)}\\\\
\dfrac{10}{\sin(34\degree)} &= \dfrac{AB}{\sin(81\degree)}\\\\
\dfrac{10\sin(81\degree)}{\sin(34\degree)} &=AB\\\\
17.7&\approx AB
\end{align}$

###Answer

$AB\approx 17.7$

[[☃ image 1]]

options.alt: Triangle ABC. Side AC measures 10 units. Angle A is 65 degrees and angle C is 81 degrees.

hints:
###Strategy

$\dfrac{\text{length opposite }\angle 1}{\sin(m\angle 1)}=\dfrac{\text{length opposite }\angle 2}{\sin(m\angle 2)}$

###Selecting two angles and their opposite sides

We know that $m\angle A=98\degree$ and that the opposite side measures $15$ units. To solve for $AB$, we'll need the measure of the opposite angle, $\angle C$.

$\begin{align}
m\angle A+m\angle B + m\angle C&=180\degree\\\\
98\degree+ 45\degree + m\angle C&=180\degree\\\\
m\angle C&=37\degree
\end{align}$

###Applying the law of sines

$\begin{align}
\dfrac{BC}{\sin(m\angle A)} &= \dfrac{AB}{\sin(m\angle C)}\\\\
\dfrac{15}{\sin(98\degree)} &= \dfrac{AB}{\sin(37\degree)}\\\\
\dfrac{15\sin(37\degree)}{\sin(98\degree)} &=AB\\\\
9.1&\approx AB
\end{align}$

###Answer

$AB\approx 9.1$

[[☃ image 1]]

options.alt: Triangle ABC. Side BC measures 15 units. Angle A is 98 degrees and angle B is 45 degrees.

assessment_items.question_content:
The following figure shows $\triangle ABC$ with side lengths to the nearest tenth.

[[☃ image 1]]

**Find $BC$ in $\triangle ABC$.**  
Round to the nearest tenth.

$BC={}$[[☃ numeric-input 1]]
hints:
###Strategy

We can use the **law of sines** to relate the side lengths and angle measures of a triangle when we know the measures of either of the following:

- Two sides and a *non-included* angle
- Two angles and any side (though we might need to find the other angle measure)

The law of sines says the following is true for any pair of angles in a triangle:

$\dfrac{\text{length opposite }\angle 1}{\sin(m\angle 1)}=\dfrac{\text{length opposite }\angle 2}{\sin(m\angle 2)}$

###Selecting two angles and their opposite sides

We know that $m\angle C=24\degree$ and that the opposite side measures $5$ units. To solve for $BC$, we'll need the measure of the opposite angle, $\angle A$.

$\begin{align}
m\angle A+m\angle B + m\angle C&=180\degree\\\\
m\angle A+130\degree + 24\degree&=180\degree\\\\
m\angle A&=26\degree
\end{align}$

###Applying the law of sines

$\begin{align}
\dfrac{BC}{\sin(m\angle A)} &= \dfrac{AB}{\sin(m\angle C)}\\\\
\dfrac{BC}{\sin(26\degree)} &= \dfrac{5}{\sin(24\degree)}\\\\
BC&=\dfrac{5\sin(26\degree)}{\sin(24\degree)}\\\\
BC &\approx 5.4
\end{align}$

###Answer

$BC\approx 5.4$

[[☃ image 1]]

options.alt: Triangle ABC. Side AB measures 5 units. Angle B is 130 degrees and angle C is 24 degrees.

assessment_items.question_content:
The following figure shows $\triangle ABC$ with side lengths to the nearest tenth.

[[☃ image 1]]

**Find $BC$ in $\triangle ABC$.**  
Round to the nearest tenth.

$BC={}$[[☃ numeric-input 1]]
hints:
###Strategy

We can use the **law of sines** to relate the side lengths and angle measures of a triangle when we know the measures of either of the following:

- Two sides and a *non-included* angle
- Two angles and any side (though we might need to find the other angle measure)

The law of sines says the following is true for any pair of angles in a triangle:

$\dfrac{\text{length opposite }\angle 1}{\sin(m\angle 1)}=\dfrac{\text{length opposite }\angle 2}{\sin(m\angle 2)}$

###Selecting two angles and their opposite sides

Side $\overline{BC}$ is opposite $\angle A$, which measures $42\degree$. We know that side $\overline{AC}$ measures $16$ units. To apply the law of sines, we'll need the measure of $\angle B$, the angle opposite side $\overline{AC}$.

$\begin{align}
m\angle A+m\angle B + m\angle C&=180\degree\\\\
42\degree+m\angle B + 39\degree&=180\degree\\\\
m\angle B&=99\degree
\end{align}$

###Applying the law of sines

$\begin{align}
\dfrac{AC}{\sin(m\angle B)} &= \dfrac{BC}{\sin(m\angle A)}\\\\
\dfrac{16}{\sin(99\degree)} &= \dfrac{BC}{\sin(42\degree)}\\\\
\dfrac{16\sin(42\degree)}{\sin(99\degree)} &=BC\\\\
10.8&\approx BC
\end{align}$

###Answer

$BC\approx 10.8$

[[☃ image 1]]

options.alt: Triangle ABC. Side AC measures 16 units. Angle A is 42 degrees and angle C is 39 degrees.

assessment_items.question_content:
The following figure shows $\triangle ABC$ with side lengths to the nearest tenth.

[[☃ image 2]]

** Find $m\angle A$. **  
*Note that $m\angle A$ is acute. Round to the nearest degree.*

$m\angle A=$ [[☃ numeric-input 1]]$\degree$
hints:
###Strategy

We can use the **law of sines** to relate the side lengths and angle measures of a triangle when we know the measures of either of the following:

- Two sides and a *non-included* angle
- Two angles and any side (though we might need to find the other angle measure)

The law of sines says the following is true for any pair of angles in a triangle:

$\dfrac{\sin(m\angle 1)}{\text{length opposite }\angle 1}=\dfrac{\sin(m\angle 2)}{\text{length opposite }\angle 2}$

###Selecting two sides and their opposite angles

We know that $m\angle C=52\degree$ and that the opposite side measures $21$ units. Angle $\angle A$ is opposite side $\overline{BC}$, which measures $13$ units.

We can substitute these values into the law of sines equation and solve for $m\angle A$.

*However*, as long as $m\angle A$ isn't exactly $90\degree$, there will be two possible solutions to the equation: one acute and one obtuse. The inverse sine function gives us the acute solution, while the obtuse solution will be its supplementary angle.

[[☃ explanation 1]]

In this case, we know $\angle A$ is acute.

###Applying the law of sines

$\begin{align}
\dfrac{\sin(m\angle A)}{BC}&=\dfrac{\sin(m\angle C)}{AB}\\\\
\dfrac{\sin(m\angle A)}{13}&=\dfrac{\sin(52 \degree)}{21}\\\\
\sin(m\angle A)&=\dfrac{13\sin(52\degree)}{21}\\\\
\sin(m\angle A)&\approx 0.4878\\\\
m\angle A&\approx \sin^{-1}(0.4878)\\\\
m\angle A&\approx 29\degree
\end{align}$

We know that $\angle A$ is supposed to be acute, so we can be certain that its measure is $29\degree$.

###Answer

To the nearest degree, $m\angle A \approx 29\degree$.

[[☃ image 1]]

options.alt: Triangle ABC. Side AB measures 21 units and side BC measures 13 units. Angle C is 52 degrees.

answers.message: Angle A is acute. Also, this would make the interior angle measures of the triangle add up to more than 180 degrees.

assessment_items.question_content:
The following figure shows $\triangle ABC$ with side lengths to the nearest tenth.

[[☃ image 1]]

** Find $m\angle C$. **  
*Note that $m\angle C$ is acute. Round to the nearest degree.*

$m\angle C=$ [[☃ numeric-input 1]]$\degree$
hints:
###Strategy

We can use the **law of sines** to relate the side lengths and angle measures of a triangle when we know the measures of either of the following:

- Two sides and a *non-included* angle
- Two angles and any side (though we might need to find the other angle measure)

The law of sines says the following is true for any pair of angles in a triangle:

$\dfrac{\sin(m\angle 1)}{\text{length opposite }\angle 1}=\dfrac{\sin(m\angle 2)}{\text{length opposite }\angle 2}$

###Selecting two sides and their opposite angles

We know that $m\angle B=68\degree$ and that the opposite side measures $18$ units. Angle $\angle C$ is opposite side $\overline{AB}$, which measures $7$ units.

We can substitute these values into the law of sines equation and solve for $m\angle C$.

*However*, as long as $m\angle C$ isn't exactly $90\degree$, there will be two possible solutions to the equation: one acute and one obtuse. The inverse sine function gives us the acute solution, while the obtuse solution will be its supplementary angle.

[[☃ explanation 1]]

In this case, we know $\angle C$ is acute.

###Applying the law of sines

$\begin{align}
\dfrac{\sin(m\angle C)}{AB}&=\dfrac{\sin(m\angle B)}{AC}\\\\
\dfrac{\sin(m\angle C)}{7}&=\dfrac{\sin(68 \degree)}{18}\\\\
\sin(m\angle C)&=\dfrac{7\sin(68\degree)}{18}\\\\
\sin(m\angle C)&\approx 0.3606\\\\
m\angle C&\approx \sin^{-1}(0.3606)\\\\
m\angle C&\approx 21\degree
\end{align}$

We know that $\angle A$ is supposed to be acute, so we can be certain that its measure is $21\degree$.

###Answer

To the nearest degree, $m\angle C \approx 21\degree$.

[[☃ image 1]]

options.alt: Triangle ABC. Side AB measures 21 units and side BC measures 13 units. Angle C is 52 degrees.

answers.message: Angle C is acute. Also, this would make the interior angle measures of the triangle add up to more than 180 degrees.

assessment_items.question_content:
The following figure shows $\triangle ABC$ with side lengths to the nearest tenth.

[[☃ image 1]]

** Find $m\angle A$. **  
*Note that $m\angle A$ is acute. Round to the nearest degree.*

$m\angle A=$ [[☃ numeric-input 1]]$\degree$
hints:
###Strategy

We can use the **law of sines** to relate the side lengths and angle measures of a triangle when we know the measures of either of the following:

- Two sides and a *non-included* angle
- Two angles and any side (though we might need to find the other angle measure)

The law of sines says the following is true for any pair of angles in a triangle:

$\dfrac{\sin(m\angle 1)}{\text{length opposite }\angle 1}=\dfrac{\sin(m\angle 2)}{\text{length opposite }\angle 2}$

###Selecting two sides and their opposite angles

We know that $m\angle B=28\degree$ and that the opposite side measures $8$ units. Angle $\angle A$ is opposite side $\overline{BC}$, which measures $15$ units.

We can substitute these values into the law of sines equation and solve for $m\angle A$.

*However*, as long as $m\angle A$ isn't exactly $90\degree$, there will be two possible solutions to the equation: one acute and one obtuse. The inverse sine function gives us the acute solution, while the obtuse solution will be its supplementary angle.

[[☃ explanation 1]]

In this case, we know $\angle A$ is acute.

###Applying the law of sines

$\begin{align}
\dfrac{\sin(m\angle A)}{BC}&=\dfrac{\sin(m\angle B)}{AC}\\\\
\dfrac{\sin(m\angle A)}{15}&=\dfrac{\sin(28 \degree)}{8}\\\\
\sin(m\angle A)&=\dfrac{15\sin(28\degree)}{8}\\\\
\sin(m\angle A)&\approx 0.8803\\\\
m\angle A&\approx \sin^{-1}(0.8803)\\\\
m\angle A&\approx 62\degree
\end{align}$

We know that $\angle A$ is supposed to be acute, so we can be certain that its measure is $62\degree$.

###Answer

To the nearest degree, $m\angle A \approx 62\degree$.

[[☃ image 1]]

options.alt: Triangle ABC. Side AC measures 8 units and side BC measures 15 units. Angle B is 28 degrees.

answers.message: Angle A is acute. Also, this would make the interior angle measures of the triangle add up to more than 180 degrees.

hints:
###Strategy

- Two sides and a *non-included* angle

$\dfrac{\sin(m\angle 1)}{\text{length opposite }\angle 1}=\dfrac{\sin(m\angle 2)}{\text{length opposite }\angle 2}$

###Selecting two sides and their opposite angles

We know that $m\angle A=48\degree$ and that the opposite side measures $20$ units. Angle $\angle B$ is opposite side $\overline{AC}$, which measures $26$ units.

We can substitute these values into the law of sines equation and solve for $m\angle B$.

*However*, as long as $m\angle B$ isn't exactly $90\degree$, there will be two possible solutions to the equation: one acute and one obtuse. The inverse sine function gives us the acute solution, while the obtuse solution will be its supplementary angle.

[[☃ explanation 1]]

In this case, we know $\angle B$ is acute.

###Applying the law of sines

$\begin{align}

assessment_items.question_content:
The following figure shows $\triangle ABC$ with side lengths to the nearest tenth.

[[☃ image 1]]

** Find $m\angle C$. **  
*Note that $m\angle C$ is obtuse. Round to the nearest degree.*

$m\angle C=$ [[☃ numeric-input 1]]$\degree$
hints:
###Strategy

We can use the **law of sines** to relate the side lengths and angle measures of a triangle when we know the measures of either of the following:

- Two sides and a *non-included* angle
- Two angles and any side (though we might need to find the other angle measure)

The law of sines says the following is true for any pair of angles in a triangle:

$\dfrac{\sin(m\angle 1)}{\text{length opposite }\angle 1}=\dfrac{\sin(m\angle 2)}{\text{length opposite }\angle 2}$

###Selecting two sides and their opposite angles

We know that $m\angle A=30\degree$ and that the opposite side measures $19$ units. Angle $\angle C$ is opposite side $\overline{AB}$, which measures $37$ units.

We can substitute these values into the law of sines equation and solve for $m\angle C$.

*However*, as long as $m\angle C$ isn't exactly $90\degree$, there will be two possible solutions to the equation: one acute and one obtuse. The inverse sine function gives us the acute solution, while the obtuse solution will be its supplementary angle.

[[☃ explanation 2]]

In this case, we know $\angle C$ is obtuse.

###Applying the law of sines

$\begin{align}
\dfrac{\sin(m\angle C)}{AB}&=\dfrac{\sin(m\angle A)}{BC}\\\\
\dfrac{\sin(m\angle C)}{37}&=\dfrac{\sin(30\degree)}{19}\\\\
\sin(m\angle C)&=\dfrac{37\sin(30\degree)}{19}\\\\
\sin(m\angle C)&\approx 0.9737\\\\
m\angle C&\approx \sin^{-1}(0.9737)\\\\
m\angle C&\approx 77\degree
\end{align}$

We know that $\angle C$ is supposed to be obtuse, so its measure must be $180\degree-77\degree=103\degree$.

###Answer

To the nearest degree, $m\angle C \approx 103\degree$.

[[☃ image 1]]

options.alt: Triangle ABC. Side AB measures 37 units and side BC measures 19 units. Angle A is 30 degrees.

hints:
###Strategy

We can use the **law of sines** to relate the side lengths and angle measures of a triangle when we know the measures of either of the following:

- Two sides and a *non-included* angle

The law of sines says the following is true for any pair of angles in a triangle:

###Selecting two sides and their opposite angles

We can substitute these values into the law of sines equation and solve for $m\angle A$.

*However*, as long as $m\angle A$ isn't exactly $90\degree$, there will be two possible solutions to the equation: one acute and one obtuse. The inverse sine function gives us the acute solution, while the obtuse solution will be its supplementary angle.

In this case, we know $\angle A$ is obtuse.

$\begin{align}

** Find $m\angle B$. **  
*Note that $m\angle B$ is obtuse. Round to the nearest degree.*

$m\angle B=$ [[☃ numeric-input 1]]$\degree$
hints:
###Strategy

We can use the **law of sines** to relate the side lengths and angle measures of a triangle when we know the measures of either of the following:

- Two sides and a *non-included* angle
- Two angles and any side (though we might need to find the other angle measure)

The law of sines says the following is true for any pair of angles in a triangle:

$\dfrac{\sin(m\angle 1)}{\text{length opposite }\angle 1}=\dfrac{\sin(m\angle 2)}{\text{length opposite }\angle 2}$

###Selecting two sides and their opposite angles

We know that $m\angle C=29\degree$ and that the opposite side measures $9$ units. Angle $\angle B$ is opposite side $\overline{AC}$, which measures $18$ units.

We can substitute these values into the law of sines equation and solve for $m\angle B$.

*However*, as long as $m\angle B$ isn't exactly $90\degree$, there will be two possible solutions to the equation: one acute and one obtuse. The inverse sine function gives us the acute solution, while the obtuse solution will be its supplementary angle.

[[☃ explanation 1]]

In this case, we know $\angle B$ is obtuse.

###Applying the law of sines

$\begin{align}
\dfrac{\sin(m\angle B)}{AC}&=\dfrac{\sin(m\angle C)}{AB}\\\\
\dfrac{\sin(m\angle B)}{18}&=\dfrac{\sin(29\degree)}{9}\\\\
\sin(m\angle B)&=\dfrac{18\sin(29\degree)}{9}\\\\
\sin(m\angle B)&\approx 0.9696\\\\
m\angle B&\approx \sin^{-1}(0.9696)\\\\
m\angle B&\approx 76\degree
\end{align}$

We know that $\angle B$ is supposed to be obtuse, so its measure must be $180\degree-76\degree=104\degree$.

###Answer

To the nearest degree, $m\angle B \approx 104\degree$.
